content.problemStatement:
Chef currently has A1A_1A1​ gold coins and B1B_1B1​ silver coins with him.

He can perform the following three types of transformations:


Pay 111 gold coin and receive 555 silver coins.

This can only be done if he has at least one gold coin.
Pay 555 silver coins and receive 111 gold coin.

This can only be done if he has at least five silver coins.
Discard one gold coin and one silver coin.

This can only be done if he has at least one gold coin **and** at least one silver coin.
Is it possible, using these transformations, for Chef to end up with exactly A2A_2A2​ gold coins and B2B_2B2​ silver coins?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains 444 space-separated integers A1,B1,A2,A_1, B_1, A_2,A1​,B1​,A2​, and B2B_2B2​ — the initial and target number of gold and silver coins.


Output Format
For each test case, output the answer on a single line: YES\text{YES}YES if it's possible to reach A2A_2A2​ gold and B2B_2B2​ silver coins, and NO\text{NO}NO otherwise.


Each character of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e. the strings NO,No,nO,\text{NO}, \text{No}, \text{nO},NO,No,nO, and no\text{no}no will all be treated as equivalent.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We already have A1=A2A_1 = A_2A1​=A2​ and B1=B2B_1 = B_2B1​=B2​, so nothing needs to be done.
Test case 222: It can be shown that it's impossible to obtain 111 gold coin and 555 silver coins starting with 222 gold and 111 silver coin.
Test case 333: One valid sequence of operations is:

We start with 333 gold coins and 222 silver coins.
First, discard one gold and one silver coin. The counts of gold and silver are now 222 and 111.
Next, pay one gold coin and obtain five silver coins. The counts of gold and silver are now 111 and 666.
Next, discard one gold and one silver coin. The counts of gold and silver are now 000 and 555, as desired.
